text: The surgeon releases free air through the umbilical port and establishes pneumoperitoneum at twelve millimetres of mercury. Three additional laparoscopic ports are inserted: a ten-millimetre port at the umbilicus, a five-millimetre port at the left iliac fossa, and a five-millimetre port at the right upper quadrant.

The surgeon explores the site of perforation on the transverse colon, marked by a visible tattoo. The perforation is assessed as an AAST grade II injury, less than four centimeters in length, well vascularized, and mobile. The margins of the perforation are freshened, with minimal contamination observed in the prepared bowel.

The surgeon cleans the perforation margin with a 4x3 inch swab, soaking up blood. Intracorporeal suturing of the colonic perforation is performed using 3-0 PDS sutures in an interrupted, full-thickness manner. The first two sutures are placed at the far and near ends, then pulled through the 10mm port on the right side as stay sutures. Three additional sutures are placed, including one at the furthermost end. The sutures are tied off with intracorporeal knots, starting with the far end, followed by the near end, and finally the middle sutures.

The surgeon ties off the remaining sutures using intracorporeal knot tying with C and reverse C techniques, without the needle attached. Thorola Bhaj is performed starting at the perforation site, moving to the left upper quadrant, central inter-loop area, pelvis, right side of the abdomen, right sub-hepatic and supranic areas, and returning to the perforation site. A size twenty-eight Wallis drain is placed and secured on the left side of the abdomen.
